correctness.justification: All 24 acceptance tests pass. The implementation correctly handles valid emails (simple, subdomain, plus-tag, minimal, special chars, numeric), invalid emails (no @, no local part, leading/trailing dots, consecutive dots, domain issues, multiple @), length boundaries (exactly 254 chars valid, 255 invalid), and structural edge cases (numeric TLD, hyphen-ending label, empty label between dots).

edge_cases.justification: All edge cases pass. The implementation handles: empty string, missing local part, leading/trailing dots in local, consecutive dots, domain leading dot, domain without dot, single-char TLD, multiple @ signs, hyphen-start/end domain labels, empty labels between dots, numeric-only TLD, and exact length boundary conditions (254 vs 255 chars).

code_quality.justification: Clean, well-structured code with full type hints, descriptive naming, and good separation of concerns (helper functions _is_valid_local and _is_valid_domain). Pre-compiled regex patterns at module level is idiomatic and efficient. The split into private helpers improves readability. Minor note: the _LABEL_PATTERN handles single-char labels correctly via the optional group, which is subtle but correct.

documentation.justification: The main validate_email function has a comprehensive docstring with Parameters, Returns, and Examples sections using NumPy-style formatting. Helper functions have brief but adequate docstrings. Module-level docstring clearly states scope and RFC compliance disclaimer. The only minor gap is that helper functions lack parameter/return documentation in the same detail as the public function.

test_coverage.justification: Tests are well-organized with clear section comments (happy path, spec edge cases, length boundaries, structural edge cases). Covers 6 valid email variants, 11 invalid cases, 2 length boundary tests, and 3 structural edge cases. Tests are specific and meaningful. Minor gap: no tests for very short valid emails at the lower boundary or for whitespace-containing inputs.

robustness.justification: Input validation is solid: checks for empty/None-ish strings, enforces max length, validates exactly one @ sign, validates local and domain parts separately with regex. The regex patterns are pre-compiled for efficiency. The function gracefully returns False for all invalid inputs rather than raising exceptions, which is appropriate for a validation utility. Minor gap: no explicit handling of non-string inputs (e.g., None or integers would raise AttributeError).
